Introduction to MPCurver

Intro.Rmd
library(MPCurver)
set.seed(42)

Overview

MPCurver fits smooth trajectory models for pseudotime inference in high-dimensional data. Given an n×dn \times d data matrix, the package recovers a latent ordering of the nn samples by fitting a Gaussian mixture model whose component means are constrained to vary smoothly along an ordered axis.

The main entry point is fit_mpcurve(), which now exposes a CAVI-first public interface. Legacy smooth_em and csmooth_em routines remain available as lower-level functions for historical comparison, but are not part of the recommended wrapper workflow.

The model

We observe a data matrix X=(xij)∈ℝn×d. X = (x_{ij}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}.

The MPCurve model posits:

  1. Each sample ii has a latent position ti∈{1,…,K}t_i \in \{1,\dots,K\} along an ordered grid of KK components.
  2. Each feature jj has a smooth trajectory uj=(uj1,…,ujK)⊤u_j = (u_{j1},\dots,u_{jK})^\top across the KK components.
  3. Observations follow a Gaussian likelihood: xij∣ti=k,uj,σj2∼N(ujk,σj2). x_{ij} \mid t_i = k,\; u_j,\; \sigma_j^2 \;\sim\; N(u_{jk},\; \sigma_j^2).
  4. Each trajectory is regularised by a Gaussian Markov random field (GMRF) prior: uj∼N(0,(λjQK)−1), u_j \sim N\!\bigl(0,\; (\lambda_j\, Q_K)^{-1}\bigr), where QKQ_K is a random-walk precision matrix on the KK grid points and λj>0\lambda_j > 0 controls the smoothness of feature jj.

Both σj2\sigma_j^2 and λj\lambda_j are feature-specific and are estimated from the data.

The algorithm

MPCurver uses coordinate-ascent variational inference (CAVI). The posterior over latent positions and trajectories is approximated by a mean-field family: q(T,U)=∏i=1nqi(ti)∏j=1dqj(uj), q(T,U) = \prod_{i=1}^n q_i(t_i)\;\prod_{j=1}^d q_j(u_j), where qj(uj)=N(mj,Sj)q_j(u_j) = N(m_j, S_j).

CAVI maximises the evidence lower bound (ELBO) one block at a time. Each coordinate update has a closed-form solution:

Block Update
Trajectories q(uj)q(u_j) Sj=(λjQK+diag(Nk/σj2))−1S_j = (\lambda_j Q_K + \mathrm{diag}(N_k/\sigma_j^2))^{-1}, mj=Sjbj\;m_j = S_j b_j
Positions q(ti)q(t_i) rik∝πkexp(−12∑j[(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kk]/σj2)r_{ik} \propto \pi_k \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_j [(x_{ij}-m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}]/\sigma_j^2\bigr)
Mixing weights πk=Nk/n\pi_k = N_k / n
Feature variance σj2=1n∑i,krik[(xij−mjk)2+Sj,kk]\sigma_j^2 = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i,k} r_{ik}\bigl[(x_{ij}-m_{jk})^2 + S_{j,kk}\bigr]
Smoothness λj=rank(QK)/(mj⊤QKmj+tr(QKSj))\lambda_j = \mathrm{rank}(Q_K)\big/\bigl(m_j^\top Q_K m_j + \mathrm{tr}(Q_K S_j)\bigr)

Because every update is an exact coordinate maximiser, the recorded elbo_trace is guaranteed to be non-decreasing.

A key feature of the CAVI updates is the posterior uncertainty term Sj,kkS_{j,kk}, which appears in both the position update and the variance update. This is what distinguishes the variational approach from a simpler plug-in EM algorithm.

Example: fitting a spiral

We simulate a 2D spiral with 1000 samples.

sim <- simulate_swiss_roll_1d_2d(
  n = 1000,
  t_range = c(1.5 * pi, 4.5 * pi),
  sigma = 0.12,
  seed = 1
)

X <- as.matrix(sim$obs)
t_true <- sim$t

cat("Data dimensions:", nrow(X), "x", ncol(X), "\n")
#> Data dimensions: 1000 x 2
pal <- grDevices::colorRampPalette(
  c("#1F3A5F", "#2E8A99", "#F2C14E", "#E76F51")
)(256)
col_true <- pal[cut(t_true, breaks = 256, labels = FALSE)]

plot(X, col = col_true, pch = 19, cex = 0.55,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "y",
     main = "Observed spiral (coloured by true pseudotime)")

Simulated spiral coloured by true pseudotime.

Fitting

The main function is fit_mpcurve(). We choose K = 60 grid points and the Fiedler (spectral) initialisation:

fit <- fit_mpcurve(
  X,
  method = "fiedler",
  K = 60,
  iter = 120
)

fit
#> MPCurve model fit
#>   Algorithm : cavi
#>   Model     : homoskedastic
#>   n / d / K : 1000 / 2 / 60
#>   Iterations: 46
#>   ELBO (last)  : -4787.638294
#>   logLik (last): -4474.988366

Convergence

The ELBO should be monotonically non-decreasing:

stopifnot(all(diff(fit$elbo_trace) >= -1e-8))
plot(fit, plot_type = "elbo")

ELBO trace showing monotone convergence.

Ordering recovery

We can extract a pseudotime estimate from the posterior responsibilities: t̂i=∑k=1Kkrik. \hat{t}_i = \sum_{k=1}^K k\, r_{ik}. 

pseudotime <- as.numeric(fit$gamma %*% seq_len(ncol(fit$gamma)))
rho <- abs(cor(pseudotime, t_true, method = "spearman"))

cat("Spearman correlation with true pseudotime:", round(rho, 4), "\n")
#> Spearman correlation with true pseudotime: 0.9998
col_fit <- pal[cut(pseudotime, breaks = 256, labels = FALSE)]

op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(X, col = col_true, pch = 19, cex = 0.5,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "y", main = "Truth")
plot(X, col = col_fit, pch = 19, cex = 0.5,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "y", main = "CAVI fit")

True pseudotime (left) vs inferred pseudotime (right). 

par(op)

Inferred trajectories

The posterior mean trajectories mjm_j show how each feature changes along the ordering:

plot(fit, plot_type = "mu")

Posterior mean trajectories for each feature across the K grid points.

Key parameters

Parameter Default Description
K min(50, floor(n/5)) Number of grid points along the trajectory
method "PCA" Initialisation ordering: "PCA", "fiedler", "isomap", "tSNE", "pcurve", "random"
rw_q 2 Random-walk order for the GMRF prior (1 = piecewise linear, 2 = piecewise cubic)
iter 100 Maximum number of CAVI sweeps
lambda 1 Initial value for the smoothness parameters λj\lambda_j

Fixing lambda

By default, the smoothness parameter λj\lambda_j is estimated for each feature via the variational update. You can fix it at a constant value using do_mpcurve():

# Continue fitting with lambda fixed at 10
fit_fixed <- do_mpcurve(fit, iter = 50, lambda = 10)

Or restrict the optimisation range:

# Allow lambda to optimise within [1, 100]
fit_bounded <- do_mpcurve(fit, iter = 50, lambda_min = 1, lambda_max = 100)

Initialisation

The choice of initialisation method can matter, especially for complex geometries. You can compare multiple initialisations in parallel:

fits <- fit_mpcurve(
  X,
  method = c("PCA", "fiedler", "isomap"),
  K = 60,
  iter = 100,
  num_cores = 3
)

# Compare final ELBO values
attr(fits, "summary")

Rules of thumb:

  • "PCA" works well when the underlying trajectory is roughly linear.
  • "fiedler" (spectral ordering) is a good default for moderate non-linearity.
  • "isomap" can be better for strongly curved manifolds.

Continuing a fit

Use do_mpcurve() to run additional iterations on an existing fit:

fit <- do_mpcurve(fit, iter = 50)

This preserves and extends all traces (elbo_trace, loglik_trace, lambda_trace, etc.), so you can monitor convergence incrementally.

Summary

  • fit_mpcurve() is the main entry point.
  • The CAVI algorithm maintains full posterior uncertainty over feature trajectories, with a monotone ELBO guarantee.
  • Use plot() to visualise scatterplots, ELBO traces, and trajectory profiles.
  • Use do_mpcurve() to continue fitting, fix lambda, or restrict parameter bounds.
  • For dual-ordering / feature partition problems, see vignette("partition").

On this page
